a: Around 3000 BCE this ruler unified Upper and Lower Egypt as the first of his kind
notes: Egypt was unified c.3000 BCE under the first Pharaoh, beginning dynastic rule that built the pyramids.

a: Led the Hebrew Exodus out of Egypt and received the Ten Commandments on Mt. Sinai
notes: c.1200s BCE Moses led the Exodus and codified Hebrew law as the Mosaic covenant.

a: This prince founded Buddhism in India around 500 BCE
notes: c.500 BCE Siddhartha Gautama founded Buddhism, later spread by Ashoka after 250 BCE.

a: Born in the Levant at year 0, his followers built the Catholic Church
notes: Born c.0 in the Levant; told St. Peter to build his church, who became the first Pope.

a: Chosen as the last Prophet in the line of Abraham, Moses and Jesus; received the Quran
notes: c.600 Muhammad received the Quran, made the hijra from Mecca to Medina, and united the Arab tribes.

a: Macedonian king who spread Hellenistic culture from Egypt to Mesopotamia by 300 BCE
notes: Conquered from Greece to India; at his death (323 BCE) his generals split the empire (Ptolemy took Egypt).

a: Founded the largest contiguous land empire in history beginning in 1206
notes: The Mongol Empire (1206-1368) transformed Eurasian warfare and trade and overran much of the Islamic world.

a: Departed Spain in 1492 with three ships seeking a westward route to Asia
notes: His 1492 voyage launched Spanish overseas colonies and European colonization of the Americas.

a: Led nonviolent resistance and the Salt March against British rule in India
notes: Led the Non-Cooperation (1920) and Salt March (1930) movements; central to Indian independence in 1947.

a: Delivered the 'I Have a Dream' speech at the 1963 March on Washington
notes: Led the Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955) era; the March on Washington drew over 200,000 people.

a: Crossed the Alps with war elephants to invade Italy in the Second Punic War
notes: Crushed Rome at Cannae (216 BCE) but was defeated by Scipio at Zama (202 BCE).

a: Defeated Hannibal at the Battle of Zama in North Africa in 202 BCE
notes: His victory at Zama ended the Second Punic War and forced Carthage to surrender.

a: Crossed the Rubicon in 49 BCE, ended the Roman Republic, and declared himself Dictator
notes: His crossing ended the Republic; his heir Octavian later founded the Empire.

a: Defeated Marc Antony and Cleopatra, then declared the Roman Empire in 27 BCE
notes: Became Rome's first emperor as Augustus, beginning the Pax Romana.

a: Converted to Christianity after the Battle of the Milvian Bridge and moved the capital east
notes: Converted in 312, legalized Christianity (Edict of Milan, 313), and founded Constantinople (330).

a: Crowned 'Emperor of the Romans' by the Pope in 800, the first since Rome's fall
notes: United the medieval Christian West; his empire split at the Treaty of Verdun into France and Germany.

a: Founded Daoism around 600 BCE, emphasizing harmony with the Tao
notes: Daoism became one of China's foundational philosophies alongside Confucianism.

a: Taught ethics and social order in China around 500 BCE
notes: Confucian thought shaped Chinese bureaucracy and governance for over two millennia.

a: Posted the 95 Theses against the Catholic Church, sparking the Protestant Reformation
notes: In 1517 his 95 Theses (provoked by indulgence abuses) launched the Reformation; Lutheranism followed.

a: Published the Principia Mathematica, a foundation of modern physics, in 1687
notes: His Principia was a cornerstone of the Scientific Revolution.

a: Halted the Umayyad Caliphate's northern advance at the Battle of Tours in 732
notes: Stopped the Muslim advance into Frankish lands and began the Carolingian line that led to Charlemagne.

a: Sunni Ayyubid leader who eliminated the Shia Fatimids and retook Jerusalem from the Crusaders
notes: In 1187 Saladin retook Jerusalem; he faced Richard I in the Third Crusade, which ended in truce.

a: Norman duke who won the Battle of Hastings in 1066, linking feudal England to Europe
notes: His victory at Hastings brought Viking-descended Norman rule and feudal ties to England.

a: Mongol khan who ended the Abbasid Caliphate by sacking Baghdad in 1258
notes: Massacred Baghdad and burned the House of Wisdom, ending the Abbasid Golden Age.

a: Used Aristotelian logic to reconcile Christian theology with Greek reason in the 1200s
notes: His Scholasticism brought Greek epistemological authority to religious conclusions.

a: Wrote al-Qanun (The Canon), the medical textbook taught in European schools for centuries
notes: Combined Greek and Islamic medicine; his Canon dominated European medical education for centuries.

a: Conquered the Aztec Empire in Mexico beginning in 1519
notes: Toppled the Aztec Empire, establishing Spanish colonial rule in Mexico.

a: Conquered the Inca Empire in Peru in 1532
notes: His conquest of the Inca extended the Spanish Empire across South America.

a: Led the first global circumnavigation voyage in 1519, though he died en route
notes: His expedition completed the first circumnavigation of the globe.

a: Reached India by sea around Africa in 1498, creating a direct spice route
notes: His Portuguese voyage opened a direct maritime spice route to India.

a: United the Anglo-Saxons against Viking invasions; his grandson became first King of England
notes: Alfred (800s) united Angles and Saxons against the Vikings; grandson Aethelstan became first King of England in 927.

a: Grandson of Alfred who became the first King of the Kingdom of England in 927
notes: Consolidated the Anglo-Saxon kingdoms into a single English crown.

a: Wrote Leviathan (1651), arguing for a strong sovereign to escape the chaotic 'state of nature'
notes: Written during the English Civil War; foundational to social-contract theory.

a: Wrote Two Treatises of Government (1689) on consent of the governed and natural rights
notes: Argued government derives legitimacy from the consent of the governed; rights to life, liberty, property.

a: Proposed dividing government into executive, legislative, and judicial branches in 1748
notes: His 'Spirit of the Laws' shaped modern constitutional separation of powers.

a: Wrote 'The Social Contract' (1762) on popular sovereignty and the general will
notes: Argued the general will of the people is the true source of political authority.

a: Published On the Origin of Species (1859), introducing natural selection
notes: Argued all species descend from a common origin via natural selection, transforming biology.

a: Published 'A Short Account of the Destruction of the Indies' (1552) denouncing colonial brutality
notes: Pioneered arguments for Indigenous rights against Spanish colonial cruelty.

a: Published 'Democracy in America' (1835), warning of the risks of equality and majority rule
notes: His analysis of US institutions weighed the strengths and risks of democracy.

a: Socialist who won nearly a million votes for president and was jailed for antiwar activity
notes: Led the Socialist Party of America; imprisoned under the Espionage Act for antiwar speech.

a: Turkic Mamluk sultan who held off both the Mongols and the Crusaders from Cairo
notes: c.1260 the Mamluks overthrew the Ayyubids; Baibars defended Islam against Hulagu's Mongols and the Crusaders.

a: Warlord of one Seljuk tribe who established the Ottoman Empire around 1299
notes: After the Seljuk Sultanate fragmented, Osman's principality grew into the Ottoman Empire (1299-1922).

a: Andalusian scholar who, with Maimonides, commented on Greek texts via structured debate
notes: 900s-1200s Andalusian scholars promoted reason, pluralism, and the recovery of Aristotle.

a: Scottish anatomist of the 1830s whose racial theories fueled early scientific racism
notes: Popularized comparative anatomy claims that dark-skinned peoples 'could never be civilized.'

a: Journalist who coined the term 'Manifest Destiny' in 1845
notes: Framed US westward expansion as inevitable and divinely justified.

a: Leaked the Pentagon Papers in 1971, revealing government deception about Vietnam
notes: His leak exposed official lies about the progress of the Vietnam War.

a: Founded the Mughal Empire in northern India in 1526
notes: His dynasty ruled India until Aurangzeb's death (1707) fragmented the empire.

a: First emperor who unified China and built the Great Wall under the Qin Dynasty in 221 BCE
notes: Unified warring China; the succeeding Han Dynasty built Confucian bureaucracy and the Silk Road.

a: Launched China's market-reform era beginning in 1978
notes: His reforms opened China economically after Mao's Cultural Revolution.

a: Mauryan emperor who converted to Buddhism around 250 BCE and spread it widely
notes: His patronage spread Buddhism across India and beyond.

a: The completion of Khufu's monument at Giza in 2560 BCE during the Fourth Dynasty
notes: Built c.2560 BCE; the Pyramids of Khafre and Menkaure completed the Giza complex.

a: In 476 Odoacer overthrew Romulus Augustulus, collapsing this western state
b: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
notes: Germanic tribes fragmented the empire; roads, schools, and coinage decayed across Europe.

a: Around 1350 this pandemic killed roughly one-third of Europe's population
notes: The largest plague since the Plague of Justinian (541) also killed about a third of the population.

a: The 1492 transatlantic crossing that began European colonization of the Americas
notes: Launched the Age of Exploration and Spain's first global maritime empire (1492-1898).

a: The 1789 upheaval that toppled Louis XVI, brought the Reign of Terror and Robespierre
notes: Produced the Declaration of the Rights of Man; the brutal secular Republic persecuted dissenters.

a: The 1914 assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand that ignited the First World War
notes: Triggered WWI; the harsh Treaty of Versailles (1919) later planted the seeds of WWII.

a: Japan's 1941 surprise attack that brought the United States into World War II
notes: Japan attacked the US naval base in 1941; the US entered the war the next day.

concept: Events — End of the Cold War
a: The 1989 fall that preceded the 1991 collapse of the Soviet Union
notes: Symbolized the end of the Cold War; the USSR collapsed two years later.

a: The 1861 conflict that began over slavery and states' rights in the United States
notes: Led to the Emancipation Proclamation (1863) and the 13th Amendment (1865) abolishing slavery.

a: The 1975 event that ended the Vietnam War and unified the country under communism
notes: North Vietnam achieved reunification; the US had withdrawn in 1973 amid public backlash.

a: The 264 BCE war between Rome and Carthage over control of Sicily
notes: Carthage surrendered Sicily; two more Punic Wars followed, ending in Carthage's destruction (146 BCE).

a: In 216 BCE Hannibal decisively crushed a larger Roman army in this Italian battle
notes: One of history's worst Roman defeats; Hannibal's tactical masterpiece during the Second Punic War.

a: In 202 BCE Scipio Africanus defeated Hannibal in North Africa, ending the Second Punic War
notes: Carthage surrendered, ceding Mediterranean dominance to Rome.

a: The 1066 battle where William the Conqueror's Normans defeated the English
notes: Linked feudal England to continental Europe under Norman rule.

a: In 1096 Pope Urban II sent Christian knights to capture Jerusalem from Islam
notes: Aimed to unite the Eastern Orthodox church and seize Jerusalem; the first of nine Crusades.

a: In 732 Charles Martel stopped the Umayyad Caliphate's northern advance at this battle
notes: Halted the Muslim advance into Frankish Europe and began the Carolingian rise.

a: In 410 the Visigoths under Alaric sacked this imperial city
notes: A shocking blow that signaled the decline of Western Roman power.

a: In 378 the Eastern Roman army was crushed by the Goths in this battle
notes: A devastating defeat that exposed Rome's vulnerability to Gothic forces.

a: The 325 church council convened by Constantine that defined core Christian doctrine
notes: Produced the Nicene Creed, standardizing Christian belief.

a: The 1330s-1450s conflict in which England's king claimed the French throne
notes: A long dynastic struggle between England and France over the French crown.

a: The 312 battle after which Constantine converted to Christianity
b: The Battle of the Milvian Bridge
notes: His victory and conversion led to the legalization of Christianity in 313.

a: The 1202 crusade in which Western crusaders instead sacked Constantinople
notes: Crusaders turned on the Byzantine Empire, sacking the Christian capital.

a: The 1453 Ottoman conquest that ended the Byzantine Empire
notes: Ottoman forces took the city, ending the thousand-year Byzantine Empire.

a: The 1258 Mongol assault that destroyed Baghdad and the House of Wisdom
notes: Hulagu Khan's Mongols ended the Abbasid Caliphate and the Islamic Golden Age.

a: The 1757 battle that secured Bengal and made the British East India Company a territorial power
notes: Gave the Company control over Bengal's army, taxes, and courts, beginning colonial rule in India.

a: The 1815 battle where Britain and allies defeated Napoleon, beginning Pax Britannica
notes: Britain became global hegemon; France restored its monarchy amid further revolutions.

a: The 1943 battle where the Soviet Union won a decisive victory over Germany
notes: Turned the tide on the Eastern Front after Germany's 1941 invasion of the USSR.

a: The 1944 Allied landings in Normandy that began the liberation of Europe
notes: Opened the Western Front and began the push to liberate Nazi-occupied Europe.

a: The 1773 Massachusetts protest against British taxation without representation
notes: Followed the Boston Massacre (1770); provoked the Intolerable Acts and colonial unity.

concept: Events — Shot Heard Round the World
a: The 1775 battles that marked the start of the armed American Revolution
b: The Battles of Lexington and Concord
notes: Began open warfare between colonial militias and British troops.

a: The 451 battle where Romans and Visigoths together repelled Attila the Hun
b: The Battle of the Catalaunian Plains
notes: A rare late-Roman alliance halted the Hunnic advance into Gaul.

a: The 1759 battle that helped Britain capture Canada during the global Seven Years' War
b: The Battle of the Plains of Abraham
notes: Part of Britain's worldwide 1759 victories over France; sealed by the Treaty of Paris (1763).

a: The 1857 Indian rebellion that ended East India Company rule and began the British Raj
notes: Fueled by resentment of Company rule; the Crown dissolved the Company and ruled India directly.

a: The 1919 massacre where British troops fired on unarmed civilians, radicalizing the independence movement
notes: Also called the Jallianwala Bagh Massacre; catalyzed anti-colonial sentiment in India.

a: The 1968 North Vietnamese offensive in Saigon that shocked US public opinion
notes: Though militarily repelled, it revealed the war's stalemate and surged the antiwar movement.

a: The 1964 naval incident used to justify large-scale US intervention in Vietnam
notes: Congress passed a resolution granting LBJ broad war powers.

a: The 1968 massacre whose leak fueled US public outrage and antiwar sentiment
notes: Its exposure strengthened the antiwar movement against US leadership.

a: The 1877 nationwide strike, sparked by wage cuts, that federal troops crushed
notes: Began in West Virginia and spread nationwide; troops killed dozens ending it.

a: The 1892 strike at Carnegie Steel crushed by private security and state forces
notes: A violent defeat for organized labor during the Gilded Age.

a: The 1894 strike that halted rail transport until federal troops broke it
notes: Federal intervention ended the strike, leading to dozens of deaths.

a: The 132 revolt in Judea crushed by Rome, causing massive casualties and diaspora
notes: Rome's suppression scattered the Jewish population across the empire.

a: The crisis beginning in 235 of civil war, plague, and invasions that nearly destroyed Rome
b: The Crisis of the Third Century
notes: Diocletian's reforms (284) and the Tetrarchy later stabilized the empire.

a: The 1676 Virginia rebellion uniting enslaved Africans, servants, and poor whites against the governor
notes: Its multiracial alliance prompted elites to entrench racial divisions to prevent unity.

a: The 1787 uprising of indebted farmers that pushed elites toward a stronger federal government
notes: Marchers on the Springfield arsenal alarmed elites, prompting the Constitutional Convention.

a: The 1831 Virginia rebellion led by an enslaved preacher who read an eclipse as a divine sign
notes: Turner and followers killed 55; militias killed over 100 Black people in reprisal.

a: The 1886 Chicago labor rally that ended in violence after a bomb, stigmatizing the movement
notes: Damaged the public image of the labor movement amid Gilded Age unrest.

a: The 1770 famine under British East India Company rule that killed about 10 million
notes: Occurred under Company rule; a later 1943 Bengal Famine killed at least 3 million.

a: The 1592 war in which Japan invaded Korea and Ming China intervened
notes: Ming China intervened to repel the Japanese invasion of Korea.

a: The 1467 war that sparked Japan's Sengoku period of civil war
notes: Plunged Japan into a century of warring-states conflict until Tokugawa unification.

a: The 1850 rebellion that killed millions and weakened the Qing Dynasty
notes: One of history's deadliest civil wars, exposing Qing weakness alongside the Opium Wars.

a: The faith beginning c.2100 BCE with Abraham's migration from polytheistic Mesopotamia
notes: Abraham's covenant established a divine-law basis for justice, the root of the Abrahamic worldview.

a: The faith spread by early communities under Roman persecution that challenged imperial authority
notes: Organized through charity networks; legalized in 313 and made it possible to claim one true emperor.

a: The faith founded by Muhammad that united the Arab tribes into an Islamic state
notes: Spread by the Caliphates (622-1258), unifying vast regions with Arabic culture and law.

a: The belief system founded by Siddhartha Gautama in India around 500 BCE
notes: Spread across East Asia; Mahayana Buddhism flourished under the Tang and earlier dynasties.

a: The system of ethics and social order taught by Confucius around 500 BCE
notes: Underpinned Chinese bureaucracy and governance for over two thousand years.

a: The faith forming from Vedic texts and Brahmanism in India around 1000 BCE
notes: Developed from Indo-Aryan Vedic religion; the Upanishads shifted focus to moksha and karma.

a: The system of citizen assemblies formalized in Athens around 500 BCE
notes: Cleisthenes (508 BCE) created direct democracy with assemblies and ostracism to check tyranny.

a: The cultural rebirth catalyzed in 1400s Italy as Byzantine scholars fled west
notes: Byzantine scholars fleeing to Italy revived classical learning and the arts.

a: The 1750s French movement of reason, secularism, and progress, embodied by the Encyclopedie
notes: Championed reason and progress, shaping revolutionary ideals across the West.

a: The transformation from 1750 Britain that drove urbanization, factory labor, and class divisions
notes: Reshaped economies and societies, creating new class structures and labor conditions.

a: The Chinese philosophy founded by Laozi emphasizing harmony with the natural way
notes: Founded c.600 BCE, it became a foundational Chinese philosophy alongside Confucianism.

a: The native Japanese spirituality of myths and rituals developing around 500 BCE
notes: Japan's indigenous spiritual tradition of kami, myths, and rituals.

a: The movement sparked by Luther's 95 Theses against the Catholic Church
notes: Sparked religious pluralism and reshaped the relationship between church and state.

a: The movement sparked when Copernicus challenged the cosmos in 1543
notes: Galileo, Kepler, Descartes, Bacon, and Boyle pioneered modern scientific methods.

a: The decentralized medieval system where kings gave land to elites in exchange for service
notes: Kings gave land to elites, who gave money to knights and security to serfs, forming alliances around land for security.

a: The movement (Benedictines) from c.500 that preserved classical texts and promoted literacy
notes: Monasteries preserved classical learning and innovated in agriculture and education.

a: The doctrine the Zhou Dynasty introduced in 1046 BCE to justify overthrowing the Shang
notes: Held that rulers govern by divine approval, which could be lost through misrule.

a: The series of religious wars (1096-1281) between Christianity and Islam over the Holy Land
notes: Nine campaigns from the First Crusade (1096) onward; also revived European contact with Arabic science.

a: The 1845 belief that the US had a divine mission to expand westward across the continent
notes: Framed territorial expansion as inevitable and morally justified.

a: The 1940s-60s movement against Jim Crow segregation through protest and legal challenge
notes: Won landmark victories like Brown v. Board (1954) and the Civil Rights Act (1964) against entrenched racism.

a: The 1200s method that reconciled Christian faith with Aristotelian logic
notes: Thinkers like Aquinas, Averroes, and Maimonides reconciled faith with reason.

a: The c.660 schism over whether the Caliph should be elected or come from the Prophet's family
notes: Sunnis held the Caliph should be chosen; Shia held it should stay in the Prophet's family (Ali).

a: The 1054 split between Latin Roman Catholicism and Eastern Greek Orthodoxy
notes: Divided Christianity into Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox churches.

a: The 750-1250 era when Baghdad was a global center for poets, physicians, and astronomers
notes: Greek texts were translated into Arabic and scholarship flourished until the Mongol sack of Baghdad.

a: The economic idea, enforced by the 1651 Navigation Acts, tying colonies' trade to the mother country
notes: Treated the global market as finite, binding colonial trade to Britain.

a: The theory that government legitimacy derives from the consent of the governed
notes: Developed by Hobbes, Locke, and Rousseau as the basis of legitimate political authority.

a: The Roman doctrine that universal moral principles exist above human-made law
notes: Roman jurists articulated that universal moral principles stand above human law.

a: The centuries of Christian campaigns to retake Iberia from Islamic rule, ending in 1492
notes: The Fall of Granada (1492) completed the Christian reconquest of Spain.

a: The legal doctrine holding that a married woman's legal identity was 'covered' by her husband
notes: Denied married women property, contracts, wages, and guardianship until reform organizing in the 1800s.

a: FDR's 1933 program of public works and regulation responding to the Great Depression
notes: Stabilized capitalism through relief and regulation but often excluded marginalized groups.

a: The c.500 BCE school linking mathematics, harmony, and philosophy, influencing Plato
notes: Pythagoras's school connected number, harmony, and cosmos, shaping later Greek thought.

a: The Greek culture spread across Egypt, Persia, and Asia by Alexander's conquests
notes: Greek scholarship spread into North Africa, later recovered through Arabic translation.

a: The cultural revival catalyzed by Charlemagne's support of learning around 800
notes: The first of several pre-Renaissance revivals (Carolingian, Ottonian, 12th-century, Italian).

a: The 1200s system of church-run courts to investigate and punish heresy
notes: After defending Europe's borders against Islam, the Church turned inward to persecute Jews and Muslims.

a: The Spanish 'purity of blood' laws (1478) treating religion as an inheritable, biological trait
notes: The first time white supremacy was codified into law; exported to colonies as racial caste systems.

a: The 1500-1600 development of modern banking, stock exchanges, and accounting
notes: Laid the groundwork for capitalism; the Dutch VOC (1602) had widely tradable shares.

a: The 1900-1917 US reforms of antitrust laws, labor protections, and direct elections
notes: Roosevelt and Wilson advanced reforms responding to Gilded Age inequality.

a: The 1905 organization promoting militant labor organizing across all industries
b: The Industrial Workers of the World (IWW)
notes: Organized workers across industries; faced fierce repression during the Red Scares.

a: The Indian movement of boycotting British goods, sparked by the 1905 Partition of Bengal
notes: Protested Britain's divide-and-rule partition of Bengal by boycotting British goods.

a: Gandhi's 1920-22 campaign of mass protests, boycotts, and strikes against British rule
notes: Made Gandhi a national leader through mass civil disobedience.

a: The c.600 BCE Levantine tradition of prophets as moral critics of rulers
notes: An early foundation for holding leaders accountable to divine moral mandate.

a: The tradition begun in 586 BCE Babylon of formal oral commentary on Torah law
notes: Exile-era commentary laid the groundwork for the Talmudic tradition.

a: The principle emphasized by Aristotle that laws, not rulers, should govern
notes: c.500 BCE Greek philosophers argued government should be bound by law, not personal will.

a: The c.200 BCE Roman system balancing consuls, the Senate, and popular assemblies
notes: Balanced monarchy, aristocracy, and democracy, an early model of checks and balances.

a: The 1800s use of pseudoscience to justify racial hierarchy and dehumanization
notes: Figures like Robert Knox used comparative anatomy to claim racial inferiority.

a: The 1823 US doctrine opposing European colonization of the Americas, later stretched to justify intervention
notes: Issued in 1823; over time it evolved from continental defense into a tool for US intervention and dominance.

a: The 1947 US policy, via the Truman Doctrine, to fight the global spread of communism
notes: The Truman Doctrine and Marshall Plan aimed to halt the spread of communism.

a: Mao's 1966 movement targeting elites and intellectuals, devastating traditional systems
notes: Followed the Great Leap Forward; targeted intellectuals until Deng's reforms began in 1978.

a: Mao's 1958 campaign of rapid industrialization that devastated traditional systems
notes: Mao's drive for rapid modernization caused massive disruption and famine.

a: The doctrine embodied by Louis XIV of 17th-century France: 'L'etat, c'est moi'
notes: Louis XIV (personal rule from 1661) concentrated total sovereignty in the monarch; later challenged by constitutional revolutions.

a: The 1754 BCE Babylonian code of public laws with proportional punishment
notes: Established that laws must be written and publicly accessible, with proportional punishment.

a: The codified moral and civil law given to Moses on Mt. Sinai around 1200 BCE
notes: Codified moral and civil law as a covenant between people and God, with equal accountability.

a: The 1215 charter forced on King John, the first limitation on absolutist monarchy
notes: Introduced due process, trial by peers, no taxation without consent, and oversight by 25 barons.

a: The 1776 document proclaiming ideals of equality and independence from Britain
notes: Proclaimed equality even as slavery and property-based voting persisted among its signers.

a: The 1787 document creating a strong federal government superseding the states
notes: Strengthened federal power after Shays' Rebellion, though racial and property exclusions persisted.

a: The first ten amendments to the US Constitution, ratified in 1791, guaranteeing civil liberties
notes: Codified the most expansive set of civil rights up to that point in history.

a: The Hebrew written law fully codified as supreme legal and moral authority by 500 BCE
notes: Its canonization in Jerusalem made written law the supreme legal and moral authority.

a: The text Muhammad received as God's revelation, central to Islam
notes: Revealed to Muhammad c.600; the foundational scripture of Islam.

a: The 451 BCE first codified Roman laws, publicly posted for patricians and plebeians alike
notes: Rome's first written law code, ensuring legal equality between patricians and plebeians.

a: The 1919 treaty whose harsh terms on Germany planted seeds for the Great Depression and WWII
notes: Its punishing terms on Germany helped set the stage for World War II.

a: The 621 BCE first written Athenian law code, known for harsh penalties applying to all citizens
notes: Athens's first written code; its severity gave us the word 'draconian.'

a: The 594 BCE Athenian reforms granting debt relief and broader political participation
notes: Provided debt relief, wider participation, and a legal appeal process in Athens.

a: The 508 BCE Athenian reforms creating direct democracy with assemblies and ostracism
notes: Established direct democracy with citizen assemblies and ostracism to check tyranny.

a: The 529 Byzantine code (Corpus Juris Civilis) consolidating centuries of Roman law
notes: Emperor Justinian's code influenced European civil law and Enlightenment thinkers.

a: The 622 agreement establishing multi-faith governance and mutual defense in Medina
notes: Provided binding agreements between ruler and ruled, reflected in later ideas of religious freedom.

a: The 1689 document establishing parliamentary supremacy and banning cruel punishments
notes: Institutionalized Parliament and individual liberties after the Glorious Revolution.

a: The 1777 US draft creating a weak federal government lacking taxation and enforcement power
notes: Their weakness (no taxation, regulation, or enforcement) led to the stronger 1787 Constitution.

a: Lincoln's 1863 proclamation declaring freedom for slaves in Confederate states
notes: Declared freedom for enslaved people in Confederate states during the Civil War.

a: The 1865 amendment that abolished slavery in the United States
notes: Abolished slavery, shifting reform toward citizenship and rights for all.

a: The 1648 peace ending the Thirty Years' War and establishing state sovereignty
notes: Ended the Thirty Years' War and established sovereignty and non-interference.

a: The c.1900 BCE covenant in Canaan establishing a divine-law basis for justice and moral order
notes: Established a divine-law basis for justice, foundational to the Abrahamic faiths.

a: The c.1250 BCE expansion of Mosaic law into detailed religious, ethical, and civil obligations
notes: Expanded Mosaic law into a detailed code of religious, ethical, and civil duties.

a: The 313 edict that legalized Christianity in the Roman Empire
notes: Changed the church-state relationship; one God made one true emperor possible.

a: The statement of core Christian doctrine produced at the Council of Nicaea in 325
notes: Defined core Christian doctrine to standardize belief across the empire.

a: The 843 treaty splitting Charlemagne's empire into the future France and Germany
notes: Divided the Carolingian Empire among Charlemagne's heirs into West and East Francia.

a: The 1555 agreement letting Holy Roman rulers choose their region's religion: Catholic or Lutheran
notes: Allowed each ruler to set the religion of their territory, easing Catholic-Lutheran conflict.

a: The 1651 acts enforcing mercantilism by tying colonies' trade to Britain
notes: Bound colonial trade to Britain under the mercantilist idea of a finite global market.

concept: Documents — End of Seven Years' War
a: The 1763 treaty ending the global Seven Years' War, giving Britain Canada and dominance in India
notes: Britain gained Canada and India while France lost most overseas territories.

a: The 1848 treaty ending the Mexican-American War and ceding seven southwestern states to the US
notes: Ceded California and the modern Southwest (WY/UT/CO/NV/AZ/NM/CA) to the United States.

a: The 1868 amendment defining citizenship, whose 'male' voting language sparked the suffrage movement
notes: Defined citizenship; its gendered voting language helped spark women's suffrage activism.

a: The c.1450 Iroquois Confederacy charter of federal union that influenced US federalism
notes: United Six Nations in a federal union with checks and balances, influencing US federalism.

a: The 212 edict granting Roman citizenship to all free men in the empire
notes: Extended Roman citizenship to all free men across the empire.

a: The 1773 act by which Parliament first intervened to regulate the East India Company
notes: Began Parliament's transition of the East India Company toward a governing power.

a: The 1765 tax on the colonies that sparked widespread protests and boycotts
notes: Provoked colonial protests over taxation without representation.

a: The 1767 acts taxing colonial imports, intensifying resistance
notes: Taxed imports into the colonies, fueling growing resistance to British rule.

a: The 1774 acts that prompted colonial unity and the First Continental Congress
notes: Punitive measures that unified the colonies and convened the First Continental Congress.

a: The 1820 agreement drawing a line between free and slave states
notes: Drew a geographic line dividing free and slave states to balance the Union.

a: The 1870 amendment granting voting rights regardless of race but not sex
notes: Granted voting rights regardless of race, splitting abolitionists and suffragists over sex.

a: The 1920 amendment granting women the right to vote in all states
notes: Secured women's voting rights nationwide after decades of suffrage activism.

a: The 1784 act beginning dual governance: the Company administers, the British state oversees policy
notes: Began dual control of India, with the state overseeing Company administration.

a: The Roman judicial standard, codified c.100, of 'innocent until proven guilty'
notes: Roman jurisprudence codified the principle of innocent until proven guilty.

a: The Roman c.50 BCE recognition of corporations and associations as entities with legal rights
notes: Recognized corporations, municipalities, and associations as legal entities.

a: The 1713 contract giving Britain monopoly rights to supply enslaved Africans to Spanish America
notes: Granted Britain monopoly rights over the slave trade to Spanish America.

a: The 1901 measure granting the US intervention rights in Cuba despite an earlier promise of independence
notes: Imposed US intervention rights on Cuba after promising it independence.

a: The 1917 act criminalizing antiwar speech, used to imprison Eugene V. Debs
notes: Criminalized antiwar speech; the 1918 Sedition Act expanded penalties further.

a: The 1933 New Deal act creating the FDIC and splitting commercial from investment banking
notes: Created the FDIC to insure deposits and separated commercial and investment banking.

a: The 1935 New Deal act protecting workers' right to unionize and creating the NLRB
notes: Protected collective bargaining and created the National Labor Relations Board.

a: The 1935 New Deal act creating federal pensions and unemployment insurance
notes: Created pensions and unemployment insurance, though it excluded many minority and female workers.

a: The 1938 New Deal act establishing minimum wage, a maximum workweek, and banning most child labor
notes: Set the minimum wage and maximum workweek and banned most child labor.

a: The 1964 act prohibiting segregation and discrimination in public facilities and employment
notes: Outlawed segregation and discrimination; the 1965 Voting Rights Act followed.

a: The 1965 act outlawing literacy tests and discriminatory voting practices
notes: Banned literacy tests and other discriminatory barriers to voting.
